feed.title: Micromass Athenæum
feed.subtitle: A community space for mathematical exploration, learning, and discovery.

entry.title: Notes and Solutions: Bloch - Real Numbers and Real Analysis

link.title: Notes and Solutions: Bloch - Real Numbers and Real Analysis

entry.content:
Preface

The original intent was to make the resources for this project as accessible to everyone, no matter of age, location, and so on. I originally wanted to make this more friendly towards children, however I simply do not have enough experience with them to be able to guide them step by step. My current purpose for this project is aimed towards anyone who has already formed basic intuition in mathematics through whatever means, though they are not really needed.

The bare minimum that I expect is just being able to complete about four pages of worksheets daily, with the fourth page mostly being fun exercises taking no more than 5 minutes. Most people will have never experienced something like this for mathematics, because a whole lot of the ideas are the intuition I have gained through working with Abacuses, Euclid’s Elements, Abstract Algebra, and Real Analysis.

Organization

There are various types of pages, each serving a purpose:

  1. L - List/Letter pages. Not needed for students, but briefly discusses things I want to communicate, often unrelated to the materials in the following pages. Made solely for the purpose of writing an Introduction/Preface.
  2. R - Reading materials or lesson descriptions. Guides the student to be able to perform the problems presented in later sections.
  3. P - Basic problems that immediately follow the R page. May be used as a guide or bridge into the PS page. Presented with an answer key after the E page.
  4. PS - Building on the P pages, problems here increase in difficulty. There is no answer key for this section.
  5. E - Extra problems. Not as important as the earlier page types, but I encourage you to do them.
  6. A - Mental exercise, just for fun. If I were to force you, I would say the priority of this page is probably more than E.

When working on the worksheet, I encourage you to time yourself from the first R page to the last E page. There will always be a time bar at the bottom of page E so that you can keep track of how long you spent on each worksheet.

Counting 1

This chapter is dedicated to the basic knowledge of the abacus. We currently won’t be performing logical deductions, as they will come in two chapters.

Terminology

A 5-by-17 abacus has 5 beads and 17 columns. We call each vertical group of 5 beads a column, consisting of 1 upper bead and 4 lower beads. Each column represents a number between $0$ and $9$.

The abacus is at clear when all beads are away from the bridge - this is the position representing $0$.

The lower beads count from $1$–$4$, and the upper bead contributes either $0$ or $5$. The next step is to understand how to represent numbers of $10$ or higher.

entry.title: Contributing to Micromass Athenæum
entry.content:
Whether you want to fix a typo, write a new post, or suggest a feature, this guide walks you through the full contribution workflow for Micromass Athenæum.

1. Clone the Repo and Build Locally

First, make sure you have Ruby and Bundler installed. Then:

git clone https://github.com/microfrosty/math-server.github.io.git
cd math-server.github.io
gem install bundler
bundle install
bundle exec jekyll serve

Open http://localhost:4000 in your browser. Jekyll will watch for file changes and rebuild automatically - just refresh the page to see your edits.

2. Open an Issue

Issues are how we track bugs, content requests, and feature ideas.

  1. Go to the Issues tab on GitHub.
  2. Click New issue.
  3. Give it a clear title (e.g. “Add post on the Banach–Tarski paradox” or “Fix broken MathJax on mobile”).
  4. Fill in the description - what the problem is, or what you’d like to see.
  5. Submit.

Issues are automatically added to the project board via a GitHub Actions workflow, so no manual triage needed - it’ll appear in the backlog the moment you file it.

3. Make a Pull Request

Never commit directly to main. The workflow is:

# 1. Create a branch
git checkout -b my-feature-or-post

# 2. Make your changes
# ...

# 3. Commit
git add .
git commit -m "Add post: contributing guide"

# 4. Push
git push origin my-feature-or-post

Then on GitHub:

  1. You’ll see a prompt to Compare & pull request - click it.
  2. Write a short description of what you changed and why.
  3. Link any related issue by writing Closes #42 (or whatever the issue number is) in the description. This will automatically close the issue when the PR is merged.
  4. Submit the PR and wait for review.

4. Write a New Post

All posts live in _posts/ and must be named YYYY-MM-DD-your-slug.md.

Pick the right front matter for your category

Exploration or Misc post:

---
layout: post
title: "Your Title"
subtitle: "Optional subtitle"
date: 2026-04-14
categories: [exploration]   # or [misc]
tags: [tag1, tag2]
reading_time: 6
author: "Your Name"
tldr: "One sentence summary."
---

Learning post:

---
layout: post
title: "Your Title"
subtitle: "Optional subtitle"
date: 2026-04-14
categories: [learning]
math_category: calculus     # see slugs below
tags: [calculus, limits]
reading_time: 8
author: "Your Name"
tldr: "One sentence summary."
---

Book review:

---
layout: post
title: "Review: Proofs from THE BOOK"
date: 2026-04-14
categories: [books]
book_title: "Proofs from THE BOOK"
book_author: "Martin Aigner & Günter Ziegler"
book_year: 1998
book_publisher: "Springer"
rating: 5
author: "Your Name"
tldr: "One sentence summary."
---

Available math_category slugs

calculus · linear-algebra · number-theory · topology · probability-statistics · abstract-algebra · real-analysis · discrete-mathematics

Writing math

Inline: $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$

Display:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx = \sqrt{\pi}
$$

Pre-defined macros: \R \N \Z \Q \C \eps \d

Once your post looks good locally, follow the PR steps above and it’ll be reviewed and merged into the site.
entry.summary: Whether you want to fix a typo, write a new post, or suggest a feature, this guide walks you through the full contribution workflow for Micromass Athenæum.

entry.title: Review: Basic Mathematics
entry.content:
Basic Mathematics by Lang claims to provide a firm foundation for a course in Calculus. The book is split into five parts: Algebra, Intuitive Geometry, Coordinate Geometry, and Miscellaneous.

Algebra

The Algebra portion has the reader spending most of their time proving basic properties of numbers (commutativity of addition, etc.) and very briefly covers solving linear equations with two or three unknowns and quadratic equations. Within this first part, the major issue with the book is revealed. Lang does not spend enough time on extremely relevant topics for Calculus and instead tends to spend the majority of the text proving basic properties of numbers which the reader is probably already familiar with. In addition, for someone not familiar with the material, I do not think it would be a good first experience. It is simply too brief.

Intuitive Geometry

The next part is Intuitive Geometry, which surprisingly is where the book peaks. It is extremely well written, with the only con being that it is not rigorous in certain places, which may cause frustration for some readers.

Coordinate Geometry

The next part is Coordinate Geometry, whose main purpose should be to introduce the trigonometric functions. However, Lang spends the majority of the time discussing distances between points, relating it back to the part on intuitive geometry. A large number of students struggle in Calculus because they are not familiar enough with the details of the trigonometric functions, and in this regard the book is disappointing.

Miscellaneous

The final part, titled Miscellaneous, covers functions, mappings, complex numbers, induction and summations, and determinants. This part is a little all over the place, with some chapters - such as determinants - being far too brief to be useful, and other chapters - such as mappings, which has an amazing section on permutations - being very interesting with nice questions. It is also a big positive that Lang introduces induction.

Verdict

Overall, the book varies chapter by chapter and may not be worth it for those with no experience in these topics or for those with experience in analysis already. However, for those between those skill levels, there is certainly value in working through the book, if only for the intuitive geometry portion.
entry.summary: Basic Mathematics by Lang claims to provide a firm foundation for a course in Calculus. The book is split into five parts: Algebra, Intuitive Geometry, Coordinate Geometry, and Miscellaneous. Algebra The Algebra portion has the reader spending most of their time proving basic properties of numbers (commutativity of addition, etc.) and very briefly covers solving linear equations with two or three unknowns and quadratic equations. Within this first part, the major issue with the book is revealed. Lang does not spend enough time on extremely relevant topics for Calculus and instead tends to spend the majority of the text proving basic properties of numbers which the reader is probably already familiar with. In addition, for someone not familiar with the material, I do not think it would be a good first experience. It is simply too brief. Intuitive Geometry The next part is Intuitive Geometry, which surprisingly is where the book peaks. It is extremely well written, with the only con being that it is not rigorous in certain places, which may cause frustration for some readers. Coordinate Geometry The next part is Coordinate Geometry, whose main purpose should be to introduce the trigonometric functions. However, Lang spends the majority of the time discussing distances between points, relating it back to the part on intuitive geometry. A large number of students struggle in Calculus because they are not familiar enough with the details of the trigonometric functions, and in this regard the book is disappointing. Miscellaneous The final part, titled Miscellaneous, covers functions, mappings, complex numbers, induction and summations, and determinants. This part is a little all over the place, with some chapters - such as determinants - being far too brief to be useful, and other chapters - such as mappings, which has an amazing section on permutations - being very interesting with nice questions. It is also a big positive that Lang introduces induction. Verdict Overall, the book varies chapter by chapter and may not be worth it for those with no experience in these topics or for those with experience in analysis already. However, for those between those skill levels, there is certainly value in working through the book, if only for the intuitive geometry portion.
